speaker: interviewer
text: --Weisz. Sharon?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah?

speaker: interviewer
text: Are you about-- OK. I just wanted to check your mom's spelling of her name C-L-A-I-R-E, W-E-I-S-Z.

speaker: interviewee
text: Do you need her maiden name or any of that?

speaker: interviewer
text: Only if you guys want. Survivors of the Shoah Foundation interview. October 7, 1994. Survivor Claire Weisz. Interviewer Deborah Kattler-Kupetz in Los Angeles. Interview will be conducted in English.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is your name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Claire Weisz. I was born 1923, January the 6th. My name was, that time, Claris Schoenfeld.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Munkacs, Czechoslovakia.

speaker: interviewer
text: And can you tell me a little bit about your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was the youngest. I had a brother. Eugene was his name. And I had another sister, Serene. And my second sister was Hannah, and I was the youngest. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And I had lots of aunts and uncles in Munkacs, and my grandparents were living there too. And we had a very nice family life. No matter rain or shine or snow, we had to go to see our grandparents every day.

speaker: interviewer
text: And tell me a little bit about your parents. What-- what were your parents' name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My parents' names was-- my mother's name was Czanka.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your father's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Benjamin-- Ben Schoenfeld. And he was a builder. He built the Hebrew gymnasium in Munkacs, and a lot of schools-- Czechoslovakian schools-- I mean, Czech schools. And we had in Munkacs, a-- oh, I don't know how--

speaker: interviewer
text: You went to school in Munkacs?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Munkacs, in the Hebrew gymnasium. I started there in kindergarten. And my sisters were going to different kind of schools. My sister Hannah graduated from Hungarian school. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] My brother was a builder, too.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was your grandfather in the building business also?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, my grandfather in the building business. Both my grandparents were in the building business. My mother's father was making roads in Munkacs and going to the villages. And my father was-- my father's father was a builder. He built the city hall in Munkacs, and still it's standing there the same way like it was hundreds of years ago. I don't remember the the time.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you said you had a lot of cousins and aunts and uncles?

speaker: interviewee
text: I had a lot of cousins. Five of my cousins were builders, too. And my brother was a builder, too. He built in Mexico City, part of the University of Mexico.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was your family observant or religious?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes. In Munkacs, most people were keeping holidays. My father never let working in Saturday. The workers never worked Saturday.

speaker: interviewer
text: How-- how would you describe the kind of childhood that, that you had?

speaker: interviewee
text: I had the most beautiful childhood, until the Hungarians didn't come into Munkacs back in 1937. Then started all the trouble there.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you look back on your childhood before 1937, what feelings do you have about it, or what made it so special for you?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was very special. We couldn't go out too much on the street because they beat up the Jews all the time, especially from the Hebrew school when they saw us coming out or going in. Most of them were different people from different cities when they occupied Munkacs, but some of them were the Gentiles who were born there, the same way like I, I was. They hated us, too.

speaker: interviewer
text: You mean neighbors?

speaker: interviewee
text: Our neighbors, yes. They didn't want to talk to us or to see us. If they could beat us, they were happy that they could slap one of the Jewish people.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was this before 1937 or after 1937?

speaker: interviewee
text: After 1937. Before 1937, I was friends with lot of Gentile people. We talked to them, and they were living in our street. And they were friends. They even invited me for Christmas, to see their Christmas tree, and they were friends, but not after that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember what the first thing that happened was that made the change?

speaker: interviewee
text: That we got back to Hungarian. Before, the Czech people ruled the country, and they were always democratic people and nice people, and tried to have a good culture-- like in this country-- that every child should go to school to learn how to read and write. But not the Hungarians. They didn't care about nobody.

speaker: interviewer
text: When things started to change, what did your parents tell you? How did they explain it to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Stay away from them. Try not to go on the street. Always warned us that don't be in their eyes. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] And the word dirty Jew-- that was constantly screaming dirty Jew when they saw a Jew.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember how you felt at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: How?

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you feel at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Terrible. Terrible. It was the worst thing to hear those things from people that I knew, that I'm a dirty Jew.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever think about leaving?

speaker: interviewee
text: My brother was that time-- that was already in '38-- my brother was in Spain, and he sent us papers that we should-- the whole family, my mother and all my sisters-- we should leave. But my mother didn't want to leave her parents. No way that she would go somewhere without her parents.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why was your brother in Spain?

speaker: interviewee
text: Why was he? He was studying architecture in France, and I don't know why he went at that time to Spain from France. He married there a French girl, born in Paris, already, but from Polish people, Jews. I don't know why he went to Spain, but in Spain he invented the neon sign the first time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there much discussion in your family about what to do in 1938, when things became bad for the Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes because we locked every door, and they knocked on the doors always at night and screamed, open the door. And what they could take, what they saw without asking from the tables-- candelabras, or whatever was silver there, without asking they took out. And we were quiet. We didn't say even boo.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you still in school at this time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Lots of time when we came from school-- not just me, we came always in bunches-- and it was a pleasure for them to beat us, to give us slaps some time that you can fall down for no reason at all, just to hurt us.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then what happened during that time? Tell me a little bit more about what that time was like for you.

speaker: interviewee
text: I think it was the worst time because I was a young girl, 15 year old, and I needed friends and needed to go out, not to stay in the house constantly.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did your sisters talk to you about during that time? How did they feel?

speaker: interviewee
text: They felt the same way, but we were afraid even to talk about things because they just came and banged on the doors all time, or broke even windows sometimes, just for kicks. Sometimes they didn't even come in, just broke the windows for us to scare us. And we heard every day that what they did in the city, people who had businesses, what they did. They went in. They beat up the owner of the business, and they took whatever they wanted.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened to your father's business?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father's business-- he died in 1938. He became sick. He was diabetic, and we took him-- not me-- he went to Vienna to a doctor. And he had an operation and he died in 1938 in Munkacs.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did your family do then?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing. My sisters and my mother were living-- my father was a wonderful man. He gave money to everybody, that we didn't even know whom he gave. He never mentioned it. And people came and brought the money for my mother at that time. And you know in Europe, we had-- how you call-- those tile ovens in every room. That was the heating business there, and in every room we had those ovens. And then we still had some because he always ordered a lot of that from Czechoslovakia, and we had that.

speaker: interviewee
text: So my mother and my sister Serene, they sold what we had in the house. And that's all, but I didn't miss nothing because my grandparents were living there. They were well off, too. We had food and everything. I never missed food or clothing or something. Were those your mother's parents?

speaker: interviewee
text: With my mother's parents, and also my father's parents. So I never missed nothing, like I say.

speaker: interviewer
text: So in 1938, you were telling me that that was when your father died and things were very bad for the Jews of Munkacs. And then what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: When my father passed away, it was the first thing-- he was very young at that time. And it was a terrible thing for us to bear that. He died in Vienna, and we brought him back home. And he was buried from the synagogue what my grandparents built. And it's still living, that synagogue. I saw it last year. We saw it last year. He was buried from there. And it wasn't a Jew or a Gentile who wasn't on that funeral, my father's funeral. My mother was very sad, day and night, just crying. She didn't go-- just to the cemetery. No matter what kind of snow it was, she went to the cemetery. That's it. Yeah, and then [PAUSES FOR 11 SECONDS] we went to-- you see, you could travel to Hungary without passports, without nothing because we were Hungarian. You didn't need nothing. So my sister and I-- Hannah-- we went to Hungary. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I think that was later already, when we went to Hungary. My sister was helping in the city. If there were wounded people, she helped them to put medicine on and all those things, whatever she could.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which year was this, do you think?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon me?

speaker: interviewer
text: Which year was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was in '38. My father passed away in '38, and '39 was it. Those years were very bad for all of us, when we became Hungarian.

speaker: interviewer
text: And so you were on a trip with Hannah?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you were in Budapest?

speaker: interviewee
text: And I was in Budapest. And I learned there to sew and to make patterns. And Hannah was studying how to make manicure. If we have to make a living, we should know something to do to make money. So and we did. My mother send money always for us.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were staying in Budapest with Hannah--

speaker: interviewee
text: In Budapest, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --and your mother was still with her parents and your other grandparents?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. My mother lived in her own home by herself, but my grandparents were living not far from there. But she didn't want to leave even the city. She never left since my father died.

speaker: interviewer
text: And tell me a little bit about what it was like for you and Hannah in Budapest at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: At that time, we had money and it wasn't as bad. They didn't know most of the time that we are Jews, but when the Germans came in to Budapest-- I don't know exactly when was that, the date. I think it was already 1941-- I mean, '44 I guess, or '42, something like that. It was in March, that I remember. My sister Hannah and me were in the movie theatre when the Germans occupied Budapest, and everybody was screaming in the theatre, Heil Hitler. And they got up. You didn't see nothing. It was just the red-- what they wore, the Germans, [GERMAN]. And I said to my sister, if you want to die right now, or you stand up and scream, Heil Hitler. First she didn't want to. She was a stubborn girl. And I says, please, Hannah. They will kill us right now, here. So we stood up and screamed, Heil Hitler. [PAUSES FOR 9 SECONDS] I can't even describe how we felt then between all the enemies, just people who wanted to kill us right there. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] And my mother called. We shouldn't go no places. We should stay in Budapest, because things are very bad in Munkacs. Day by day, it's worse. Don't come home. But we didn't listen. We went home. It took us 10 days on the train to go home without food, without nothing.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the plane-- the train stopped every place, and they took us down. Where you going? Where you going? Already not just Hungarians, Germans too. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] And lots of time they beat us when they took us down, just for kicks, not for a reason at all. But we made it somehow. We went back home, that they should be able to take us together with my mother and the whole family to Auschwitz. When you went home on the train with Hannah, when, when was this? Do you remember the date, around what time this was?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was some time in 1938 because my father didn't live then anymore. Just my mother was living. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I don't know exactly the date-- the month, I mean.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you went back home with Hannah, what did you think was going to happen when you found your mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: Already, we heard in Budapest were rumors that they're taking all the men for work, and they're taking the women too-- young women. We thought that my mother is not there anymore, and she wasn't old at all. But they said that they're taking not the young people. But when we got back after 10 days, my mother was there, and my grandfather and grandmother too, when they took us away.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you think this was 1944 then?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was already 1944.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you came home and you found your mother, and what did you do then, you and your sister and your mother? Were they starting to take people, or what was it like at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: We had to let Hungarian people live in our house. [? When ?] they came from the capital to live in our house, some Hungarian family were living there, but for a short time because they didn't kept us. They ate our food. Whatever we had, they took everything. [PAUSES FOR 9 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: Those times are so out from my mind, no matter how I'm thinking. I just saw my mother very sad, very sad and scared always. And my sister Serene not even talking to us, just telling us how stupid you are to come home. You were free in Budapest. Why did you come home? You didn't starve there, either. That's all. That's all. They were angry that we came home. And then what happened when you came home?

speaker: interviewee
text: Already, they gathered the Jews all over. I don't even know when the ghetto started, but I remember that we had still Pesach home. I think it was 1944. It must have been. By my grandfather's house, we had the whole family, a Seder. Then the Hungarians occupied my grandfather's house, took most of it. Just they left them. I remember that because the Hungarian army was occupied Munkacs there, so wherever they had room, they went there. I remember that, that we had the last Seder with my grandfather sometime in March. And after Pesach, they put us in the factory in the ghetto. That was-- that was the brick factory, my aunt's factory and my uncle's factory, [? Shyovitz. ?] Still, there, we didn't starve because it was there and they had some food there.
